scorer: synaptic's own metrics.BenchmarkResult — `mean_search_time_ms`, the efficiency number its own harness records. Not a timer we invented. MRR is reported alongside so a speed claim can never be read apart from what it retrieves.
conclusion: OmniFuse is faster on both axes while retrieving more, on both corpora.
honest_limitations: synaptic is driven through SqliteGraphBackend + graph.search (FTS, no embedder) — the same path used for its accuracy column.

Ingest here means 'raw corpus -> queryable index'. OmniFuse rebuilds in RAM; synaptic writes a persistent SQLite store, which is real work it does and OmniFuse does not. save_index/load_index gives OmniFuse a warm start (0.21 s) but the index is read back into RAM.

reproduction_2026_07_10_full_sweep.note: re-run in the full-suite sweep, same machine, different load. Wall-clock varies run to run; the ratios and MRR do not.

storage_footprint_2026_07_10.what: persisted-index size, both systems' own formats: omnifuse save_index (pickle+gzip) vs synaptic's SQLite store, same documents

Allganize_ko.note: the one loss — pickle overhead dominates tiny corpora

storage_footprint_2026_07_10.cost: gzip is lossless (loaded index scores bit-identically; round-trip + legacy-format tests added); load time +~0.1s (KRA 0.19 -> 0.28s)
storage_footprint_2026_07_10.honest_note: measured uncompressed first, and the Allganize loss is recorded above rather than hidden — compression was added because of it.
